structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a regional healthcare provider spanning 3 years (~36 months). Records include demographic information, clinical notes, treatment history, and discharge details for approximately 50,000 patients. Data is sourced from a single health system to minimize confounding from institutional practice variation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: readmission within 30 days of discharge. A patient is labeled positive (1) if they had an inpatient admission within 30 days following an index discharge; negative (0) otherwise. Only index discharges (first discharge in the cohort window) are included to avoid repeated measurements on the same patient. Readmission is confirmed through hospital billing and admission records.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split (no random shuffling). Training set: months 1–24 (first 24 months of data). Validation set: months 25–30 (6 months). Test set: months 31–36 (final 6 months, held-out and untouched until final evaluation). Justification: Temporal split prevents future information (readmissions or clinical events that occur after training data) from leaking into model training. This mirrors deployment reality: the model will be applied to patients at discharge time and must not have access to their post-discharge outcomes during training. The 24-month training window is sufficient for stable model fitting; the 6-month validation window allows robust hyperparameter tuning; the 6-month test window provides stable performance estimates for rare events (readmission is typically 15–25% base rate).
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). The same fitted transformers are applied without refitting to the validation and test sets. This prevents information from validation/test data from influencing preprocessing parameters, which would constitute a subtle form of leakage.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: (1) Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance type, zip code (as proxy for socioeconomic status). (2) Clinical: primary diagnosis (ICD-10), secondary diagnoses (up to 5 most frequent), admission type (emergency/planned), length of stay, comorbidity burden (Charlson index), lab values at discharge (most recent), vital signs (most recent). (3) Medication: discharge medication count, high-risk medication flags (e.g., opioids, anticoagulants). (4) Utilization history: number of ED visits in 6 months prior to index discharge, number of inpatient admissions in 12 months prior, number of outpatient visits in 3 months prior. Derived features: (a) Clinical notes: apply medical NLP pipeline (tokenization, named entity recognition for conditions/procedures) to generate bag-of-words features for chief complaints and discharge summaries; (b) Admission type × length of stay interaction; (c) Age groups (binned: 18–40, 41–65, 66–80, 80+) to capture non-linear age effects. No future information is included (e.g., readmission dates, post-discharge procedures, or clinical events after index discharge).
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: A BERT-derived architecture (e.g., Clinical BERT or SciBERT fine-tuned on clinical notes) combined with a tabular feature encoder. Architecture: (1) Embed and encode clinical notes (discharge summaries, clinical assessments) using Clinical BERT (pre-trained on biomedical text). (2) Use a separate embedding layer for structured features (demographics, labs, diagnoses). (3) Concatenate contextualized note embeddings with tabular embeddings. (4) Pass through a 2-layer transformer encoder block (8 attention heads, hidden size 256). (5) Global average pooling over the sequence. (6) Dense layers (256 → 128 → 64 → 1, with ReLU and dropout=0.3) and sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameter tuning: learning rate ∈ {1e–5, 5e–5, 1e–4}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.4}, batch size ∈ {16, 32}, trained for up to 50 epochs with early stopping (patience=5 on validation F1). Use AdamW optimizer with weight decay (1e–4).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression (L2-regularized) trained on the same feature set as the transformer model, using identical preprocessing. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. Class weights are applied to both baseline and transformer to account for imbalance (weight_neg = 1, weight_pos = count_neg / count_pos). Both models are fit on identical training data and evaluated on identical validation/test splits using the same metrics. This ensures a fair comparison; any performance difference is attributable to model architecture, not data, preprocessing, or tuning methodology.
structured_choices.primary_metric: F1-score (macro-averaged, or weighted by actual class distribution in test set). Justification: F1-score balances precision (minimizing false alarms—unnecessary interventions for low-risk patients) and recall (maximizing true positives—identifying actual high-risk patients for targeted management). This aligns with the stakeholder's goal of effective resource allocation: missing a readmission candidate (low recall) wastes intervention opportunity; flagging too many false positives (low precision) exhausts limited care management resources. F1 is more interpretable and actionable than accuracy in the presence of class imbalance. Specifically, a 5–10 percentage point improvement in F1-score (e.g., 0.65 → 0.72) would be considered clinically meaningful and justify deployment.
secondary_metrics: ROC-AUC: robustness to threshold choice and class imbalance
PR-AUC (precision-recall curve): directly sensitive to minority class performance
Sensitivity (recall at fixed threshold, e.g., 0.5): identifies what fraction of true readmissions are caught
Specificity: identifies what fraction of non-readmissions are correctly ruled out
Positive predictive value (precision): of patients flagged, how many actually readmit
Calibration: expected calibration error or Hosmer-Lemeshow test to verify predicted probabilities reflect true likelihood
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 25–30). Specifically: (1) Train the transformer and baseline on the training set. (2) Evaluate both models on the validation set using F1-score. (3) Select the hyperparameter configuration that maximizes validation F1. (4) Retrain both models on the combined training + validation data (months 1–30) using the selected hyperparameters. (5) Evaluate the final models only once on the held-out test set (months 31–36). The test set is never used for hyperparameter selection; this ensures an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held-out and locked until final model selection is complete. It is evaluated once at the end. No exploratory analysis, threshold tuning, or metric peeking occurs on the test set. This single evaluation provides the primary claim about whether the transformer outperforms logistic regression. If additional sensitivity analyses are required (e.g., performance by subgroup), they are pre-specified before test set evaluation or conducted on a separate, prospectively held-out evaluation set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds: (1) Hospital discharge practices vary across institutions and over time. Control: Confine data to a single regional health system to minimize institutional heterogeneity. Stratification check: compute F1 separately for each quarter of the temporal window to verify no time-dependent drift in performance. (2) Socioeconomic status (SES) independently affects readmission. Control: Include zip code-derived SES proxy (median income, education level by zip) as an explicit feature for both models. Interpret SES as a confounder to be adjusted for, not removed. (3) Unmeasured confounds (e.g., social support, substance abuse severity) may influence readmission independent of measured predictors. Control: This design cannot eliminate unmeasured confounds; instead, perform subgroup analysis (stratified by insurance type, race/ethnicity, age group) to check for differential model performance and explore whether confounding varies by group. Report any performance disparities. (4) Label bias: readmission ascertainment may vary (e.g., some patients seek care out-of-system, readmissions at competing hospitals may not be captured). Control: Use a single integrated health system with high capture of regional readmissions. Flag and report any known incomplete readmission capture. Sensitivity analysis: estimate bounds on performance assuming x% of true readmissions are unobserved. What the experiment establishes: that the transformer achieves higher F1 than logistic regression on this dataset, given the chosen features and temporal split. It does not establish causality or explain *why* the transformer is better. It does not establish whether the improvement generalizes to other health systems, different populations, or data collected under different EHR practices. It does not control for all possible confounds affecting readmission in the real world.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy
The experiment uses electronic health record data from a single regional healthcare provider spanning 36 months. Approximately 50,000 patient discharge records are included, each labeled for readmission within 30 days post-discharge. Data sources encompass demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance, zip code), clinical information (diagnoses, labs, vital signs, discharge medications), clinical note text (discharge summaries, clinical assessments), and readmission status confirmed through inpatient billing records.

The 30-day readmission window is clinically standard and aligns with CMS metrics. Only index discharges are used to avoid repeated measurements on the same patient. The single-system constraint minimizes confounding from institutional variation in discharge practices and ensures high fidelity in readmission ascertainment (critical for label accuracy).

**Data quality issues:** Missing values in lab results and vital signs are common; these are imputed using median values per diagnostic group (fit on training data only). Outliers in numeric features (e.g., extreme lab values) are clipped at the 99th percentile. Clinical note text quality varies; notes are preprocessed (lowercasing, stopword removal) before embedding. Readmission labels are validated through cross-referencing admission records; any ambiguous cases are flagged and excluded.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy
A strict chronological split is used to prevent data leakage and reflect deployment reality:
- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (50,000 patients, ~24 months of experience)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (6 months of new data)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (6 months of held-out data, locked until final evaluation)

**Justification:** Hospital readmission prediction is inherently temporal. The model will be deployed to score patients *at discharge time*, with access only to their pre-discharge history. A temporal split ensures training data does not include any information from after the discharge date (e.g., subsequent clinical events or readmissions), which would be unavailable at prediction time. Random splitting would violate this assumption and overestimate performance. The 24-month training window is sufficient for stable estimation of both logistic regression and transformer parameters. The 6-month validation window allows robust hyperparameter tuning for rare events (readmission rates typically 15–25%, requiring adequate positive and negative samples). The 6-month test window provides stable performance estimates with sufficient positive cases for reliable minority-class metrics (F1, recall).

### 3. Feature Engineering
**Raw features:**
- Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance type, zip code-derived SES proxy
- Clinical: primary and secondary diagnoses (ICD-10), admission type, length of stay, comorbidity burden (Charlson index), most recent lab values, most recent vital signs
- Medications: discharge medication count, flags for high-risk medications (opioids, anticoagulants)
- Utilization history: ED visits (6 months prior), inpatient admissions (12 months prior), outpatient visits (3 months prior)
- Clinical text: tokenized discharge summaries and clinical assessment notes

**Derived features:**
- NLP embeddings: Clinical BERT embeddings from discharge notes (128-dimensional contextual representations)
- Interactions: admission type × length of stay (captures risk of extended illness)
- Binned age: non-linear age effects (18–40, 41–65, 66–80, 80+)

**Preprocessing:** Numeric features (age, lab values, LOS) are standardized using z-score scaling (fit on training data only). Categorical features are one-hot encoded (fit on training). Clinical text is embedded using a pre-trained Clinical BERT model (frozen weights, no fine-tuning during this experiment). All transformers are fit on the training set and applied unchanged to validation and test sets, preventing leakage.

**No future information** is included: post-discharge diagnoses, readmission dates, or clinical events after index discharge are strictly excluded.

### 4. Model and Baseline
**Proposed model:** A hybrid transformer architecture combining clinical note embeddings with tabular features.
- Encode clinical notes using Clinical BERT (pre-trained on biomedical corpora).
- Embed tabular features (demographics, labs, diagnoses) using learned embeddings.
- Concatenate note embeddings (128-dim) with tabular embeddings (64-dim).
- Pass through a 2-layer transformer encoder (8 attention heads, 256 hidden units) to capture interactions.
- Global average pooling over sequence.
- Dense layers (256 → 128 → 64 → 1) with ReLU activations and dropout (0.3).
- Sigmoid output for binary classification.

**Hyperparameter tuning:** Search over learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.4}, batch size ∈ {16, 32}. Train for up to 50 epochs with early stopping (patience=5 on validation F1). Use AdamW optimizer with weight decay (1e-4) to prevent overfitting.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression (L2-regularized) trained on the identical feature set (after preprocessing). Logistic regression receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. Both models use class weights to handle imbalance (weight_pos = count_neg / count_pos).

**Why this baseline:** Logistic regression is the current standard for clinical risk prediction and represents the algorithmic status quo. By using identical features and preprocessing, any performance difference is attributable to model architecture (transformer capacity and attention mechanisms), not data or tuning methodology. This is the correct comparison for the stated hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric: F1-score (macro-averaged or weighted).**

**Justification:** The stakeholder goal is to reduce readmission rates through effective resource allocation. F1-score optimally balances:
- **Precision:** of patients flagged for intervention, how many actually readmit (avoids wasted resources on false positives)
- **Recall:** of true readmission cases, how many are identified (avoids missing intervention opportunities)

In class-imbalanced settings, accuracy is uninformative (a model predicting all non-readmissions achieves 75–85% accuracy). F1-score directly measures the minority-class utility. Operationally, a 5–10 percentage point improvement in F1 (e.g., 0.65 → 0.72) would justify deployment, corresponding to meaningful improvements in case identification and resource efficiency.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **ROC-AUC:** robustness across decision thresholds
- **PR-AUC:** sensitive to minority-class performance
- **Sensitivity (recall):** fraction of readmissions identified
- **Specificity:** fraction of non-readmissions correctly ruled out
- **Positive predictive value:** operational precision at clinical threshold
- **Calibration:** whether predicted probabilities match true event rates (important for risk-stratified interventions)

### 6. Validation Approach
**Model selection:** Hyperparameters are tuned by evaluating both the transformer and baseline on the validation set (months 25–30) using F1-score as the selection criterion. The configuration maximizing validation F1 is chosen for each model. After selection, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–30) using the selected hyperparameters. This maximizes training data while maintaining an unbiased test estimate.

**Test set policy:** The test set (months 31–36) is held-out and untouched until final evaluation. It is evaluated exactly once, immediately after model selection. No threshold tuning, metric peeking, or post-hoc analysis occurs on the test set. This single evaluation provides the primary claim about whether the transformer outperforms logistic regression. Any secondary or subgroup analyses are pre-specified and reported as exploratory or conducted on separate prospective hold-out data.

### 7. Controls and Confounds
**Known confounds:**
1. **Institutional variation:** Discharge practices vary across hospitals. *Control:* Confine to a single health system. Perform stratified analysis by quarter to check for time-dependent drift.
2. **Socioeconomic status:** SES independently affects readmission. *Control:* Include zip code-derived SES proxy as an explicit feature for both models. Treat SES as an adjusting covariate, not a variable to remove.
3. **Unmeasured confounds (social support, substance abuse severity):** *Control:* Perform subgroup analyses stratified by insurance type, race/ethnicity, and age to detect differential performance and confounding patterns. Report any disparities.
4. **Label bias (out-of-system readmissions):** Some patients may seek care elsewhere. *Control:* Acknowledge this limitation; perform sensitivity analysis assuming x% unobserved readmissions and report bounds on performance.

**What the experiment establishes:** That the transformer achieves higher F1-score than logistic regression on this dataset, given the chosen features, preprocessing, and temporal split. It does not establish whether the improvement generalizes to other health systems, populations, or EHR environments. It does not explain mechanistically why the transformer is better. It does not establish that the model is causally beneficial for patient outcomes—only that it predicts readmission more accurately.

**Scope of the claim:** Narrow to the specific dataset, cohort, and time period. Generalization to deployment requires additional external validation studies.
